claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperature (Ttr) follows an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreases from 101 kPa to 30 kPa for 21700 NMC LIBs at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Ttr follows an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreased from 101 to 30 kPa.

claims.claim: The time interval from safety venting to thermal runaway (tinterval) decreases from 48 s to 22 s when environmental pressure drops from 101 kPa to 30 kPa for 21700 NMC LIBs at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: tinterval reduced from 48 s to 22 s when the pressure declined from 101 to 30 kPa.

claims.claim: The maximum jet flame height of 21700 LIB thermal runaway jet fires reached its maximum value of 0.28 m at 50 kPa, and the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at any of the tested pressures.
claims.quote: the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at different pressures, and the jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m.

claims.claim: Environmental pressure has little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs, with mass loss values of 36.27 g, 39.93 g, 38.36 g, 41.70 g, and 33.16 g at 101, 90, 70, 50, and 30 kPa respectively, all at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: mass loss of 101 kPa, 90 kPa, 70 kPa, 50 kPa and 30 kPa were 36.27 g, 39.93 g, 38.36 g, 41.70 g and 33.16 g respectively. It indicated that environmental pressure has a little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs.

claims.claim: The temperature change rate (dT/dt) during thermal runaway of LIBs is related to both the internal pressure of the battery and the external environmental pressure, as derived from the heat transfer model.
claims.quote: the temperature change rate (dT/dt) is related to the internal and external pressure of the battery. Therefore, the rate of temperature rise during TR of LIBs is not only determined by the complex internal exothermic reaction but also related to the external environmental pressure.

claims.claim: T1 is the onset temperature for detectable self-heat generation in Li-ion batteries, and its mechanism is usually attributed to SEI decomposition.
claims.quote: T1 is the onset temperature for the detectable self-heat generation; the mechanism for this heat source is usually attributed to SEI decomposition.

claims.claim: A higher T2 (trigger temperature for thermal runaway) usually means better overall thermal stability, making the battery more likely to pass standard abuse tests.
claims.quote: T2 is the trigger temperature for TR. Understanding T2 is quite critical for safe battery design, because a higher T2 usually means better overall thermal stability, and the battery can thus be more likely to pass standard abuse test.

claims.claim: The total heat released during thermal runaway (∆HTR) is directly linked to the temperature difference ∆T=T3-T2, calculated as ∆HTR=M·Cp·(T3-T2), where M is cell mass and Cp is heat capacity.
claims.quote: The difference ∆T=T3-T2 is directly linked to the total heat released during a TR (∆HTR) as shown in Eqn. (1), where M is the cell mass and Cp is the heat capacity.

claims.claim: T3 (maximum temperature during thermal runaway) tends to be higher for cells with higher energy density.
claims.quote: T3 tends to be higher for cells with higher energy density (Fig. 3a).

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate (max{dT·dt-1}) reported around T2 has a high correlation with the energy density of the battery, based on ARC test data from 33 battery samples with different cell chemistries.
claims.quote: The maximum dT·dt-1 that is reported around T2 has a high correlation with the energy density of the battery (Fig. 3b).

claims.claim: In the three-stage thermal runaway process: the anode+electrolyte system generates approximately 17% of total heat in Stage I, ISC contributes only 9% of total heat in Stage II, and the cathode-anode redox reaction releases 74% of total heat in Stage III.
claims.quote: The 'anode+electrolyte' system generates approximately 17% of the total heat released during TR in Stage I... Only 9% of the total heat released during TR is caused by ISC. Stage III: The redox reaction between the cathode and anode starts and release large amount of heat, 74% in this case.

claims.claim: T2 is determined by the collapse temperature of the separator, whereas T3 is determined by the redox reaction between the cathode and anode.
claims.quote: Obviously, the T2 is determined by the collapse temperature of the separator, whereas the T3 is determined by the redox reaction between the cathode and anode.

claims.claim: When separator and electrolyte are removed from the cell (reducing mass by approximately 13%), T2 shifts to 229.4°C and T3 reaches 877.2°C, confirming that ISC does not make major contributions to total heat generation during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: T2=229.4℃, quite near to the triggering temperature, 249.2℃, of the redox reaction. T3=877.2℃ is still high, convincing us that ISC does not make major contributions in the total heat generation during TR. The higher T3 can be explained because the mass (M) of the battery is reduced by removing separator and electrolyte from the cell. The mass is reduced by approximately 13%.

claims.claim: Using only separate kinetic equations for cathode/anode reactions to model thermal runaway behavior fails to fit full-cell TR data, as the predicted T3 will be much lower than measured data due to a mismatch in the heat generation mechanism.
claims.quote: Separate kinetic equations for the cathode/anode reactions have been used to model the TR behavior [7]. However, this methodology fails to establish a model to fit the TR data for the full cell shown in Fig. 2, the predicted T3 will be much lower than the measured data, due to the mismatch in the mechanism of heat generation.

claims.claim: ISC ceases once the temperature rises to 249.2°C, when most of the electrolyte gasifies, thereby cutting off the circuit for internal short circuit.
claims.quote: But the ISC cannot release all the electric energy inside the battery, and will cease once the temperature rises to 249.2℃, when most of the electrolyte gasifies, thereby cutting off the circuit for internal short.

claims.claim: In NMC811|Gr full battery, T1 (start of exothermic side reactions) was located at 130.2 °C, T2 (uncontrollable state threshold at dT/dt = 1 °C/s) was at 206.0 °C, and T3 (maximum temperature) was 791.7 °C.
claims.quote: T1 was located at 130.2 ℃ for the NMC811|Gr full battery, indicating the starting point of exothermic side reactions... T2 of the full battery was located at 206.0 ℃... The battery temperature exponentially increased to the highest temperature of T3, which was 791.7 ℃ for the full battery.

claims.claim: For CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly partial cells, the maximum heat release rate (dT/dt)max was 101.5, 591.0, and 166.5 °C/s respectively, and T3 was 516.1, 920.3, and 476.9 °C respectively.
claims.quote: For the CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly systems, the maximum heat release rate (dT dt−1)max was 101.5, 591.0, and 166.5 ℃ s−1, respectively, while T3 was 516.1, 920.3, and 476.9 ℃, respectively

claims.claim: At T2 = 206.0 °C of the full battery, the heat release rate contributions of CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly reaction systems to the full battery were approximately 90.2%, 7.4%, and 2.4% respectively, identifying CaEly reactions as the thermal runaway triggering reaction.
claims.quote: the heat release ratios of the CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly reaction systems to the full battery at 206.0 ℃ were approximately 90.2%, 7.4%, and 2.4%, respectively... the reactions between Ca and Ely were recognized as the triggering reaction in the NMC811|Gr cell.

claims.claim: Approximately 41.2% of thermally-induced cathode oxygen reacts with EC (pathway O*/O2+EC) generating 16.7% of total heat, while the remaining 58.8% spreads to the lithiated anode (pathway O2+LiC6) generating 64.5% of total heat in the NMC811|Gr cell.
claims.quote: the pathway of O*/O2 +EC consumed 41.2% of the cathode-released oxygen with 16.7% heat generation, whereas the pathway of O2+LiC6 consumed the remaining 58.8% of the cathode-released oxygen with 64.5% heat generation

claims.claim: Using EC-free electrolyte in NMC811|Gr cells increased T2 from 206.0 °C to 235.1 °C and significantly suppressed the self-heating rate, but T3 remained high at 761.3 °C because the O2+LiC6 reaction still provided 82.5% of heat.
claims.quote: The EC-free electrolyte significantly suppressed the self-heating rate with improved T2 (increased from 206.0 ℃ to 235.1 ℃ in Fig. 4d)... T3 of the NMC811 cell_0% EC case kept the high value (761.3 ℃) because the major heat-generating reaction of O2+LiC6 still occurs provided 82.5% heat in the cell
